feedback.text: A Digital Fairness Act that delivers on the above objectives is urgently needed and I ask you to support its swift implementation. Particular attention should be paid to: (1) the right of the user to decide which software he/she will install on his device without the co-decision of the manufacturers (in Android, this is known as ‘sideloading’, but a similar issue will sooner or later also concern PCs). — the right to use purchased digital goods on a permanent basis (see e.g. the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative with 1.4 million signatures, but also many cases where software, books, music, films or other media can no longer be used by buyers). It is important that suppliers cannot circumvent consumer protection, e.g. by changing the name of the button from ‘buy’ to something else. (3) the right to resell such purchased content which, according to a judgment of the Court of Justice of the European Union, should exist but is in practice unenforceable for consumers. Rapid adaptation: If new anti-user practices are invented, there is a need to quickly improve it, otherwise the industry trivially undermines consumer rights by introducing new practices faster than the slow EU legislative process is lagging behind. (See e.g. the GDPR/GDPR, which has now been in force for almost a decade, but in the field of online advertising, most respondents are featuring and are unenforceable by the intentionally ineffective Irish authorities.)
